To  avoid  these  negative  outcomes,  the  cloud  portability  provisions  should  be  explicitly  limited  to

Under  Art.  30(1),  providers  of  infrastructure-related  services  must  ‘facilitate  the  switching  process’  by 
offering  not  only  information  and documentation,  but  also any  necessary  ‘capabilities,’  technical  support 
and tools – an obligation that is highly demanding. 

Arts 33 and 35 allow the Commission to impose harmonised standards or common specifications, a top-
down approach that overrides industry-led efforts. 

To address the excessive complexity introduced by Chapter VIII, the digital package should focus on two 
key simplifications: 

  IaaS providers should be required to enable data and workload portability using documented, 
widely adopted formats. Obligations should be limited to providing essential documentation and 
technical information, without requiring proprietary tools or bespoke migration support;16 and 

  The possibility for the Commission to impose harmonised standards or common specifications 
should  be  removed.  Instead,  consistent  with  current  Art.  33(11),  the  Commission  should  issue 
non-binding  guidelines  where  necessary,  allowing  standards  and  other  industry  initiatives  to 
evolve alongside technologies and market demand.

Digital simplification package:  
Our AI recommendations 

Setting the global standard for the regulation of artificial intelligence (AI), the AI Act represents a pivotal 
moment  for  Europe.1  As  the  world’s  most  ambitious  and  comprehensive  AI  law  worldwide,  it  has  the 
potential to both lead innovation and ensure safety. However, its complexity and breadth also make it one 
of the most challenging regulations to get right. 

Our  recommendations  aim  to  strengthen  the  AI  Act’s  global  success  through  the  upcoming  digital 
simplification  package,  by  addressing  some  of  its  most  problematic  provisions  that  risk  undermining 
Europe’s competitiveness.

2. Cookies, Consent, and Competition 

We support the Commission’s efforts to address cookie fatigue and improve transparency for 
consumers. Current consent banners often overwhelm users and fail to foster meaningful 
choice. 

However, browsers and operating systems must not become new gatekeepers. Shifting 
consent management to browsers or app stores would concentrate control in a few large 
platforms and undermine the very competition policy goals of the EU. 

Startups relying on responsible digital advertising and data-driven innovation risk being cut 
off from their users and markets. The solution lies in: 

●  Simplified, standardised consent UX that empowers users directly; 
● 
Interoperable, open consent mechanisms, governed under clear EU standards; 
●  Avoiding default blocking of third-party cookies that shuts out small publishers and 

startups from visibility and monetisation. 

 
 
 
 
 
3. Implementation of the AI Act: Timing, Guidance, and Support 

European startups are committed to developing trustworthy, safe, and innovative AI. 
However the implementation environment remains fragmented. Harmonised standards are 
not yet available, and national authorities are already interpreting early-stage obligations 
inconsistently. 

To ensure legal clarity and foster innovation, the Digital Omnibus should: 

●  Phase in high-risk AI obligations until harmonised standards and conformity 

assessment procedures are fully operational; 

●  Guarantee full alignment with the GDPR and the Data Act, preventing overlapping 

documentation, reporting, or assessment requirements; 

●  Establish an “AI Startup Compliance Sandbox” under the European AI Office, 

providing model documentation, certification templates, and practical early-stage 
guidance for startups. 

Without such targeted adjustments, the risks are significant. Uncertainty and fragmented 
enforcement could lead startups to halt AI development altogether or relocate outside the EU 
to avoid unpredictable compliance costs. A phased, supportive, and consistent 
implementation is therefore essential to maintain Europe’s competitiveness in trustworthy AI.

4. Data Access and Sharing: From Principle to Practice 

The Free Flow of Non-Personal Data Regulation works in theory, but enforcement and 
awareness remain weak. The Data Governance Act and Open Data Directive hold promise, 
yet their potential for startups is under-realised. 

Europe must move from permission to participation — making valuable public and private 
datasets actually accessible through predictable APIs and clear licensing frameworks. 

We propose: 

●  EU-wide open data registry listing accessible public datasets, with machine-readable 

metadata and harmonised standards; 

●  Cross-border reuse of public sector data for emerging sectors like climate tech, 

health, mobility, and fintech; 

●  Secure but fair access to industrial and government datasets to foster innovation 

while maintaining data protection. 

Startups can be the “translators” of open data into usable services — if they are allowed in. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
5. Cybersecurity and Reporting: Simplify to Secure 

Startups fully support the EU’s cybersecurity objectives. However, the multiplication of 
reporting obligations under NIS2, DORA, CRA, and other frameworks is creating overlap, 
cost, and confusion. 

We recommend: 

●  One-stop digital incident reporting system under ENISA for all relevant frameworks; 
●  Tiered obligations based on company size and risk profile; 
●  Practical templates and response standards co-created with SME and startup 

organisations. 

Simplification will not reduce security — it will increase compliance and resilience by making 
rules actually implementable.

Alliance for Responsible Data Collection Comments on EU Digital Simplification Package on Digital 
Regulations,  submitted October 14, 2025 

The Alliance for Responsible Data Collection (ARDC) submits the following feedback on the impact of 
Measure 1.3(1)(a) of the EU AI Act Code of Practice.  Measure 1.3(1)(A), which requires signatories to 
only use web crawlers that read and follow instructions in the Robots Exclusion Protocol (robots.txt) and 
any subsequent version the IETF demonstrates is technically feasible, is creating an anti-competitive, 
legally incoherent, and technically unworkable framework for AI governance. Its adoption lays the 
groundwork for a new category of exclusionary rights— allowing entities to privatize access to public 
web data that is neither private, copyrighted, nor copyrightable. This transforms a voluntary technical 
convention into a legal gatekeeping mechanism that threatens the open character of the web itself. 

1. Anti-Competitive Consequences
By endorsing robots.txt as an access-control mechanism, the Code of Practice risks cementing a “pay-
per-crawl” model of the internet. Under such a regime, large technology firms with financial and legal
resources will be able to negotiate individualized data-access arrangements while small and medium-
sized enterprises and non-profits are unable to compete. Humans browsing the same sites remain free
to view the data, but those who rely on automated tools for large-scale analysis must eithe

sets out 20 practical ideas to accelerate AI adoption across Europe. Among them are 
Individual AI Learning Accounts to upskill the workforce, AI Champions Networks to support 
SMEs, and a European GovAI Hub to pool public-sector AI resources. A common thread across 
many of these 20 proposals is simplification and harmonization, aimed at cutting through 
regulatory fragmentation and ensuring consistency across the Single Market. 

Our contribution below builds on these foundations and sets out five pragmatic pillars for 
simplification, each paired with concrete solutions and recommendations: 

1.  Horizontal Simplification, to streamline overlapping digital laws and reduce compliance 
duplication, e.g. through a one-year harmonization mission to align legal definitions and 
a Single Reporting Portal; 

2.  Vertical Simplification, to bridge gaps between EU and national implementation, e.g. 
through national Digital Regulation Helpdesks and systematic Competitiveness & 
Consistency Assessments of new laws to ensure alignment with EU law; 

3.  International Convergence, to align regulatory frameworks with democratic partners 

and avoid fragmentation, e.g. by aligning CEN-CENELEC AI standards with existing ISO 
and NIST frameworks, and forming a Democratic AI Forum for like-minded states; 
4.  Single Market Acceleration, to facilitate the scaling of critical enablers like data, 

compute, capital, and talent, e.g. through an AI infrastructure fast track and harmonised 
micro-credentials for AI skills portability; 

5.  SimplifAI, to use AI itself as a tool for simplification both for the public sector and for 
organizations interacting with administrations, e.g. through LLM-powered compliance 
assistants, and a “GovAI Sandbox” for experimenting with procedural agents. 

By reducing regulatory overlap, bridging EU and national layers, fostering international 
alignment, accelerating the Single Market, and using AI itself as a tool for smarter governance, 
the EU can make its digital rulebook more agile and innovation-friendly. The upcoming Digital 
Omnibus Package provides a unique opportunity to deliver on this ambition, reignite 
competitiveness, protect its citizens, and return to a path of prosperity and progress, fit for the 
Intelligence Age.

applicability of the AI Act in case the Harmonised Standards for high-risk AI systems would 
not be ready on time.  

Postponing  the  applicability  of  the  AI  Act  because  the  development  of  harmonized 
standards  is  delayed  or  for  any  other  reason,  would  undermine  both  the  purpose  and 
credibility of the regulation. The Act was designed to promote innovation and most critically 
to  address  urgent  risks  to  safety,  fundamental  rights,  and  trust  in  artificial  intelligence. 
These risks exist today, not at some distant point in the future.  

Harmonised Standards are important tools for operationalising compliance, but they are

standards evolve over time; they should not need to wait for prescriptive checklists before

The upcoming Digital Omnibus initiative presents a strategic opportunity to recalibrate Europe’s regulatory 
framework to better foster growth, innovation, and competitiveness. With this instrument, we urge the EU to 
take the approach “simple as possible, strict as necessary.” 

From SAP’s perspective, three legislative areas merit particular attention within this reform process: 

•  Data Act 

It is critical that the Omnibus includes a substantive review of the Data Act, with particular attention to 
provisions that negatively impact cloud service providers. As currently structured, the Data Act imposes 
considerable administrative burdens, regulatory overlaps, and compliance costs, while generating more risk 
and uncertainty than other data governance instruments mentioned in the Call for Evidence. A meaningful 
reduction of the regulatory burden in EU data governance requires the inclusion and revision of the Data Act. 

•  AI Act and Cyber Resilience Act 

SAP recommends delaying the application of the AI Act with respect to high-risk AI systems, as well as 
postponing the implementation of the Cyber Resilience Act, until clear and practical compliance guidance 
and European harmonised standards for presumption of conformity are available for both laws. Without such 
clarity, companies face significant uncertainty that hinders innovation, readiness, and in most severe cases – 
market access. 

SAP is concerned by the absence of any explicit reference to the Data Act or the AI Act or CRA delay in the 
Call for Evidence linked to this consultation. Excluding these three laws from the scope of the Omnibus – 
despite strong calls from EU industry for revision, clarification, or phased application – would represent a 
missed opportunity. Addressing them directly aligns with the recommendations of the Draghi report and 
would enable the Commission to strengthen its role as a champion of European economic resilience in an era 
of systemic global change.

Executive Summary  

We warmly welcome the EC willingness to simplify the digital rule book. Achieving such a 
simplification is of paramount importance to ease operators’ compliance. This is one of the key 
aspects of strengthening EU competitiveness.  

We call the EC to adopt an ambitious Digital Omnibus proposal and ensure the same approach 
will apply to the future Digital Network Act. The current European Electronic Communications 
Code also deserves a substantial simplification exercise notably in terms of consumer protection 
rules, since some of them have become obsolete or redundant with EU horizontal laws.  

The main simplifications that should be included in the Digital Omnibus, and further detailed 
below, are the following:   

AI Act  

▪  The implementation of the rules on high-risk AI should be postponed to one year after the 

standardisation process has been finalised.  

▪  We call for setting up one single certification for all harmonised standards.  
▪  The Act should ensure that a single point of contact is available at national level covering the 

various public authorities in charge of monitoring the AI Act implementation.    

ePrivacy Directive  

▪  We call for the repeal of the provisions on metadata (art.6) to ensure operators can innovate 
and manage data under the same rules (GDPR) as other digital entities, while inserting the 
principle of confidentiality of communications in another EU law (ex: DNA, GDPR).  

Data Act  

▪  The Commission should clarify the scope of the Act as definitions of “Connected Product” 
and “Connected Service” create legal uncertainty, particularly regarding whether servers, 
routers, and set-top boxes fall under mandatory data-sharing rules. The Data Act should 
explicitly exclude some products such as rented or leased devices.  

▪  Overlaps between the Data Act and the GDPR risk conflicting data rights and hindering lawful 

innovation – guidance is necessary.

• Delete Article 6(1) and Annex I to avoid automatic classification of AI components as 
high-risk or;

• Merge Sections A and B of Annex I to apply a flexible, sectoral approach and;

• Clarify the scope of AI-based safety components through targeted legal assessments.

2. Postpone the application of the AI Act for high-risk systems

• Delay the application of high-risk requirements until 12 months after harmonised 
standards are published.

• Suspend fines temporarily until harmonised standards and guidelines are in place.

3. Clarify roles and responsibilities

• Introduce a defined role for “component AI suppliers” and clarify the responsibilities 
of a "provider" to distinguish between developers and integrators.

• Recognise the original supplier of AI systems and its shared post-market obligations. 

4. Targeted amendments to make the AI Act workable in practice

• Consult Annex I for proposed targeted amendments regarding the AI Act.

Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on Digital Omnibus 

This response provides ING’s key recommendations to simplify the digital regulatory framework in 
key areas for the banking sector. Our aim is to reduce the compliance burden while maintaining 
the underlying policy objectives, including resilience as a core consideration. The 
recommendations focus on four of the ﬁve pillars that will be covered by the Digital  Omnibus: 
Data, Cybersecurity, AI, and eIDAS/Business Wallet, including the following suggestions: 

  Data: Rely on EU-level frameworks like GDPR to avoid conﬂicting national rules, clarify how

published to ensure realistic compliance timelines.

AI Act implementation 

AI  development  has  a  significant  impact  on  the  media  sector,  bringing  both  opportunities  and 
challenges. The major challenges are copyright-related. Indeed, to train and develop their models, 
AI providers have scraped copyrighted content without authorization or consideration for any opt-
outs from rightsholders, ignoring the EU copyright framework. One of the key principles of the EU 
copyright acquis is authorization. We welcome the reminder in the AI Act that 'this Regulation does 
not affect the enforcement of copyright rules as provided for under Union law' (Recital 108), as well 
as the obligation in Article 53(c) to put in place a policy to comply with Union law on copyright and 
related rights. However, the Code of Practice on Artificial Intelligence (CoP) does not allow for the 
proper application of the EU copyright regulatory framework in the context of AI. For instance, by 
favouring the application of the TDM exception rather than the opt-in principle. Furthermore, the 
draft  template  for  a  'sufficiently  detailed  summary  of  the  content  used  for  training  the  general-
purpose  AI  model'  under  Article  53(1)(d)  fails  to  provide  rightsholders  with  the  necessary 
information  to  assess  whether  their  content  has  been  used  for  training  purposes.  Moreover, 
transparency over training data is an absolute prerequisite for any remuneration negotiation and/or 
removal request. We are concerned that any simplification in the implementation of the AI Act and 
related documents would further weaken their effectiveness and lead to a watering down of EU data 
regulation,  copyright  law,  and  media  pluralism.  On  the  contrary,  it  is  crucial  to  ensure  robust 
implementation in these areas to safeguard the reliability of information and the sustainability of 
the media sector. 

MFE-MEDIAFOREUROPE N.V., 14 October 2025

1.Delay the application of High-Risk AI system requirements  

Harmonised standards for the conformity assessment of high-risk AI systems are still under development and are 
unlikely to be available before the end of 2026. Without these standards, manufacturers face legal uncertainty 
and  limited  time  to  assess  impacts,  adapt  products  and  processes,  and  train  personnel.  In  addition, 
implementation guidelines are still pending, leaving companies with unsure of how to comply or even whether 
they fall within the high-risk scope. 

CECIMO therefore urges the European Commission to:  

➢  Delay the application of high-risk AI requirements by 24 months to ensure companies have enough time 

to adopt and incorporate harmonised standards once they are published.  

➢  Suspend the enforcement of fines until those standards and guidance documents are available, to avoid 

unnecessary administrative burden and to safeguard competitiveness.  

2. Clarify roles and responsibilities  

Under the AI Act, a “provider” is defined as the person or company that places an AI system on the market under 
their  own  name  or  trademark  or  develops  it  for  their  own  use  (Article  3(3)).  In  practice,  this  means  that  a

me AI Act obligations. 

For example, Article 49 of the AI Act requires providers to register AI systems listed in Annex III 
in an EU database, regardless of whether they are ultimately deemed high risk. However, not all 
systems that fall into one of the Annex III categories will qualify as high risk AI. Where a provider 
justifies that their AI system does not pose a significant risk of harm to the health, safety or 
fundamental rights of natural persons per Article 6(3), they should not be required to register 
their system in the high risk AI database. To require them to do so would give a false impression 
that that AI system is subject to all of the high risk AI obligations. Instead, providers should only 
be expected to document their determination that the AI system is not high risk, and be 
prepared to demonstrate this rationale upon request by the competent authority. 

Another example relates to regulatory sandboxes, which are designed to support testing and 
compliance efforts by allowing companies to experiment with AI systems in a controlled 
environment. Article 57 of the AI Act states that Member State competent authorities can issue 
written proof of a successful AI regulatory sandbox. This documentation may then be "positively 
taken into account" when providers who have participated in these sandboxes wish to 
demonstrate compliance. The Digital Omnibus should specify that participating in a successful 
AI sandbox should lead to a presumption of conformity for the tested AI system or model, to 
encourage participation and streamline documentation requirements. 

3.  Clarify enforcement of the AI Act, prevent fragmentation 

The Digital Omnibus should provide additional clarity as to the role of the AI Office, and its 
remit in enforcing certain aspects of the AI Act. Where possible and practicable, the AI Office 
should play more of a role in centralised enforcement, and national fragmentation should be 
avoided. 

Regarding enforcement, the Omnibus should clarify if and how the AI Office will be involved in 
enforcing other aspects of the AI Act for which they must develop future Codes of Conduct. We 
are aware that the AI Office will work with stakeholders to develop a Transparency Code in the 
coming months, but it is not clear if they are responsible for the enforcement of this Code or 
the obligations it will aim to satisfy under Article 50 of the AI Act

d proportionate. Although newly adopted, we 
encourage the European Commission to use the opportunity of the Digital Omnibus to 
streamline and clarify some aspects of the AI Act by: 

1.  Considering practical extensions to compliance deadlines; 
2.  Acknowledging regulatory overlaps between the AI Act and other legislation, and 
suggesting methods for optimised compliance to reduce regulatory burden; 

3.  Clarifying who will be responsible for enforcement; and, 
4.  Supporting European competitiveness in AI cohesively, across regulatory initiatives. 

1.  Compliance extensions, where reasonable and practicable 

Where it seems likely that deadlines to publish standards or guidelines cannot be met by the 
publishing entity, an extension to an implementation deadline should be considered. 
Implementation timelines should be aligned with the availability of any necessary technical and 
legal guidance, so that companies can prepare accordingly. Concretely, the Digital Omnibus 
should include a proposal that stipulates that relevant AI Act rules do not apply until a number 
of months after the requisite guidelines or standards have been published. For example, if the 
development of harmonised standards for high risk AI continues to be delayed, an extension of 
the implementation deadline for High Risk AI obligations is warranted. 

2.  Acknowledge regulatory overlaps, reduce administrative burden 

The Digital Omnibus should outline where requirements in the AI Act overlap with those of 
other regulations. Where these legislative requirements have the same policy aims and 
similar obligations, the Omnibus should explain how companies can harmonise their 
approach to compliance. 

For example, Article 27 of the AI Act requires providers of high-risk AI systems to conduct 
fundamental rights impact assessments, to evaluate how an AI system may impact fundamental 
rights, including human dignity, non-discrimination and freedoms protected under the EU 
Charter. Article 35 of the GDPR simultaneously requires companies to carry out a data 
protection impact assessment (DPIA), to assess how the processing of personal data may affect 
individuals' rights and freedoms. There is some overlap between these obligations, and the

preferably using one assessment tool.

Detailed suggestions on AI, Data, Cyber, Connectivity and privacy 

Simplification of the AI Act: 

•  Continue to support and resource the AI Board and its subgroups beyond the 

implementation deadlines. These are critical regulatory forums providing guidance and 
ensuring consistency across Member States in early years of implementation.  This would 
ensure that the AI Board further coordinates with relevant digital regulation governance 
structures to ensure coherence and efficiencies across National Regulatory Authorities 
and avoid duplication of efforts.  

•  Provide greater certainty and predictability for regulators and businesses by linking 
timeline for enforcement provisions to the availability of the standards and guidance 
required to help regulate and achieve compliance. The Act is very complex, and 
implementation will be a significantly smoother process for both regulators and regulated 
entities if guidelines and harmonised standards are available well in advance of 
compliance deadlines. This will provide essential time to prepare in order to achieve 
compliance at the deadline date. 

•  Develop glossaries with common definitions including on what constitutes "putting AI 

into use" (e.g., global vs. EU-market models) or on "intended use".  

•  Clarify the interplay between the AI Act and other digital regulations, especially on 

personal data processing, on market surveillance functions and on the cooperation needed 
between market surveillance actors, notifying authorities and fundamental rights 
protection bodies to support innovation and responsible AI. This could build on the 
initiatives underway between the European Data Protection Board and the EU AI Office.  

•  Confirm the requirement for, and strengthen the mechanisms to facilitate, data sharing 
between competent authorities designated under the AI Act to enable effective and 
coherent enforcement. This is particularly important in relation to the sharing of data that 
may be considered commercially sensitive in nature. 

•  Examine and remedy legislative constraints in other sectors (e.g. financial services

At the same time, European AI regulation does not exist in isolation. Companies operating in global 
markets already engage with international initiatives such as those developed by the OECD and the 
G7. Ensuring that the AI Act’s implementation is aligned with these frameworks is essential to avoid 
creating compliance complexity or discouraging global players from deploying AI in Europe. A clear 
case for international alignment is the forthcoming Code of Practice under Article 50. The Code should 
be consistent with global standards and remain flexible in its approach to watermarking and labelling, 
allowing actors to adopt techniques that reflect their business models, the state of the art, and the 
specific  risks  posed  by  their  AI  systems.  By  actively  engaging  in  international  standardisation 
discussions,  the  EU  can  reinforce  its  role  as  a  leader  while  ensuring  European  businesses  are  not 
disadvantaged and supports transparency efforts as the necessary technology matures. 

A rational approach to exemptions 

The AI Act already contains specific exemptions based on company size, particularly to support SMEs 
and  start-ups.  While  DOT  Europe  supports  proportionality,  we  caution  against  the  introduction  of 
additional size-based carve-outs during the simplification process. The reality of the AI ecosystem is 
that it is highly interconnected: in Germany, for example, 95% of AI start-ups operate in B2B contexts, 
and across the EU, 63% of AI start-ups build on foundation models developed by larger companies. It 
is a central principle of the AI Act that there is a fair allocation of responsibility wit

es, either in their substantive provisions or in the 
envisaged dates of entry into application.  

It is indisputable that one of the core objectives of the AI Act is ensuring compliance with Union 
law by AI Actors and securing a level playing field whereby AI companies operating in the Union 
market  are  subject  to  a  common  set  of  rules,  irrespective  of  whether  those  providers  are 
established or located within the Union or in a third country. 

Against this background, EMMA and ENPA oppose in the strongest terms any potential use of the 
Digital Omnibus as a means to either dilute, in any manner, delete altogether, or postpone the 
entry into application of the copyright-relevant obligations incumbent on providers  of general-
purpose AI models per Art. 53(1)(c) and (d), which represent but an initial, yet still insufficient step 
that must necessarily be built upon.  

On the contrary, as the Commission seeks to achieve optimal implementation of the AI Act, the 
simplification  process  presents  an  opportunity  to  revisit  and  greatly  improve  the  AI  Act 
implementation  package  adopted  in  July  this  year,  comprising  the  General-Purpose  AI  (GPAI) 
Code of Practice, the AI Office’s transparency template for GPAI model providers to summarise 
the  training  content,  and  the  GPAI  guidelines,  which  were  a  missed  opportunity  to  provide 
meaningful protection of intellectual property rights in the context of generative AI and fell short 
of the EU AI Act’s own objectives.  Only in this way will the objective laid down in the AI Act of 
helping  rightsholders exercise  and  enforce their rights  under  Union  law  be achieved  and  legal 
certainty  for  all  stakeholders  be  secured,  while  also  ensuring  that  the  EU’s  AI  and  digital 
frameworks  remain  coherent,  innovation-friendly, and firmly respectful of intellectual property 
rights. 

We  thank  the  European Commission  for  the  consideration  of  our  feedback  and  remain  at  the 
Commission’s disposal for any clarifications or discussions on the raised issues.

Proportionality and avoiding cascading burdens 

Simplification must also ensure proportionality. Obligations should be phased in to allow SMEs and mid-
caps to adapt, with lighter regimes for lower-risk activities. At the same time, safeguards are needed to 
prevent the indirect transfer of compliance duties along supply chains, as observed in other fields such 
as  sustainability reporting. Without such safeguards, larger companies may cascade obligations onto 
smaller suppliers, creating disproportionate burdens and undermining the EU’s policy objectives. 

A strategic vision for digital governance 

The Digital Omnibus should not be seen as a purely technical adjustment (« simplify, just to simplify »), 
but as a step towards a strategic European digital governance model. This model should integrate 
cybersecurity,  data,  AI,  and  digital  identity  into  a  coherent,  interoperable,  and  innovation-friendly 
framework.  By  institutionalising  the  principles  of  « report  once »  and  « certify  once »,  grounding 
obligations in harmonised standards, and embracing automation and AI tools for compliance, Europe 
can strengthen both its competitiveness and its digital sovereignty.

Integrate high-risk requirements related to Annex I A 
into sectoral legislation  

Early implementation challenges are exposing the limits of applying horizontal AI rules 

to established sectoral frameworks, especially for those under Annex I section A. The 

development of harmonised standards for AI is proving slower and more complex than 

anticipated. Manufacturers are left uncertain as to how new AI-specific standards will 

align – or conflict – with existing ones that already govern their products. This 

uncertainty is bound to create bottlenecks and undermine long-standing compliance 

pathways.  

The problem is particularly acute in the area of conformity assessment. The AI Act 

introduces obligations that current conformity assessment bodies are neither clearly 

authorised nor equipped to manage under existing sectoral regimes. In highly 

regulated sectors such as automotive or medical devices, where notified bodies are 

already under strain, adding AI-related requirements without a clear integration

Bitkom recommends that ENISA conduct a comprehensive mapping of overlaps and 

inconsistencies across EU cybersecurity regulations. This analysis should rely on

Mapping EU requirements against established standards would help both authorities 

and businesses, following a risk-based approach, highlighting gaps and clarifying the

In addition, ENISA should lead the development of a harmonised cross-sectoral 

reporting framework under the CSA. Today, reporting obligations are dispersed across 

NIS2, CRA, DORA, GDPR, DA, and AI Act each with its own thresholds, timelines and 

channels. A centralised reporting portal, grounded in harmonised standards and 

coordinated by ENISA, would substantially enhance legal clarity and strengthen 

Europe’s collective ability to respond to threats. To succeed, such a framework must 

ensure interoperability, aligned definitions and standardised procedures, while 

avoiding redundant obligations and duplicate sanctions. For instance, there is 

considerable potential for harmonization when it comes to CE marking requirements. 

These should be aligned across regulatory frameworks, with a single technical file 

format recognized across frameworks and clear Commission guidance to ensure 

uniformity across Member States. By assuming a coordinating role, ENISA can ensure 

that the CSA becomes the foundation for coherence and effectiveness rather than an 

additional layer of complexity.

Article 14 of the CRA requires manufacturers to report actively exploited vulnerabilities 

through a designated platform in three stages: an early warning within 24 hours to 

both the CSIRT and ENISA, a detailed notification within 72 hours, and a final report 

within 14 days after corrective measures. This system duplicates existing frameworks 

and diverts resources from actual remediation. To improve efficiency, the reporting 

procedure should be streamlined to two steps: an initial notification within 72 hours 

with essential information and a comprehensive report within 14 days of corrective 

action. All notifications should be submitted only once, ideally through ENISA’s 

platform, to eliminate parallel processes.  

Further complexity arises from overlapping provisions in existing legislation. In 

particular, the RED Delegated Regulation (EU) 2022/30 introduces parallel 

cybersecurity requirements that duplicate the CRA. As the CRA provides a more 

comprehensive and neutral horizontal framework, the RED Delegated Act should be 

repealed once the CRA becomes applicable, especially for products covered by both 

instruments. To ensure continuity, the harmonised standards EN 18031-1/2/3 should 

be supported in such a way that existing product development processes, risk 

assessments and technical documentation can be fully leveraged for CRA compliance.

authorised nor equipped to manage under existing sectoral regimes. In highly 

regulated sectors such as automotive or medical devices, where notified bodies are 

already under strain, adding AI-related requirements without a clear integration 

pathway poses a risk for compounding delays and market disruption.  

This regulatory burden will weigh the heaviest on manufacturers in sectors where 

Europe holds longstanding competitive advantages, such as automotive, machinery or 

medical equipment.  

For these reasons, Annex I should be streamlined by merging its two sections and 

extending the more flexible Section B approach to the entire annex. This would ensure 

that AI requirements can be progressively incorporated into sectoral frameworks in a 

more stable and controlled manner, rather than applying immediately and in parallel 

with sectoral legislation. Crucially, this would allow harmonised standards for AI to be 

translated and embedded into sector-specific contexts without undermining existing 

conformity procedures.  

Integration of AI requirements into sectoral frameworks should follow a sequenced 

process grounded in existing legislation. The goal is not to reopen well-functioning 

regulatory systems, but to align them with the AI Act in a way that respects their 

structure and avoids legal uncertainty. For this approach to succeed, the AI Act 

simplification package must clarify the AI Act’s status as a maximum harmonisation 

instrument. Sector-specific measures through delegated acts, implementing acts or 

technical specifications must not impose requirements beyond the AI Act. This is 

essential to prevent inconsistent or excessive obligations, and would strengthen the 

replicability of AI harmonised standards, maintaining a unified definition of ‘state of 

the art’ across sectors.

The absence of grandfathering provisions in the EU AI Act poses a significant risk to the 

continued availability of well-established NFL-products on the EU market. These legacy

matched_paragraph:
he AI Act, with its broad, default classification of any worker-monitoring system as 'high-risk', 
fails to distinguish between invasive surveillance and protective monitoring. This blunt 
application imposes a disproportionate compliance burden, with high costs effectively barring 
smaller, specialized innovators from the market. Simultaneously, the GDPR's principle of data 
minimization is in direct conflict with the need for comprehensive datasets to train accurate 
and reliable safety-critical AI models. The conventional solution of data anonymization is often 
technically unfeasible or functionally crippling for real-time safety systems that must identify 
an individual in immediate danger. 

This report puts forward a "Blueprint for Reform" with five core, actionable recommendations 
for the Digital Omnibus and the subsequent Digital Fitness Check: 

1.  Re-calibrate the AI Act's 'High-Risk' Definition for OSH: Issue clear guidance, in 

collaboration with EU-OSHA, to create a distinction between AI for surveillance and AI for 
protection, allowing life-saving systems to be assessed on their actual risk profile rather 
than by a catch-all default. 

2.  Establish OSH-Focused AI Regulatory Sandboxes: Leverage the AI Act's sandbox 

mechanism to create dedicated, supervised environments where innovators can test and 
validate OSH technologies in collaboration with regulators and social partners, 
accelerating market access and reducing legal uncertainty. 

3.  Mandate Harmonized Standards and Professional Certification: Commission 

OSH-specific harmonized standards for AI systems to provide a clear "presumption of 
conformity" with the law, drastically reducing compliance costs. Concurrently, establish a 
"Certified AI Safety Professional" (CAISP) certification to ensure genuinely qualified 
human oversight. 

4.  Clarify Data Governance Rules for Safety Systems: Provide explicit guidance that 
recognizes Privacy-Enhancing Technologies (PETs) as a primary means of GDPR 
compliance and confirms that an employer's legal OSH obligation constitutes a valid 
basis for processing necessary health data for safety purposes. 

5.  Unify Cybersecurity Reporting: Implement a single reporting portal for cybersecurity 
incidents with a risk-based, tiered timeline that prioritizes on-the-ground safety 
response over premature administrative reporting for non-critical vulnerabilities.

Section 3: A Blueprint for Reform — Actionable 
Recommendations for the Digital Omnibus 

The analysis presented in the preceding sections demonstrates a clear and urgent need for 
targeted reforms to the EU's digital acquis. These reforms are necessary to remove the 
unintended barriers that are currently stifling the development and adoption of life-saving 
OSH technologies. This section translates that analysis into a blueprint of five concrete, 
actionable proposals for the European Commission to consider for inclusion in the Digital 
Omnibus proposal and the forthcoming Digital Fitness Check. These recommendations are 
designed not to weaken protections, but to create smarter, more nuanced, and 
technologically-aware regulations that actively foster safety innovation. 

3.1 Proposal: OSH-Focused Streams in AI Regulatory Sandboxes 

The AI Act, in Article 57, wisely provides for the establishment of "AI regulatory sandboxes" to 
foster innovation by allowing for the development, testing, and validation of AI systems in a 
controlled, supervised environment. This mechanism is the ideal tool for addressing the 
specific challenges faced by OSH AI technologies. 

Recommendation 

It is proposed that the Commission, through the Digital Omnibus or subsequent implementing 
acts, explicitly mandates or strongly encourages Member States to establish dedicated 
Occupational Safety and Health (OSH) focused streams within their national AI regulatory 
sandboxes. 

These specialized streams would create a "safe harbor" where innovative SMEs and startups 
can develop and test their technologies without the immediate threat of the severe 
administrative fines associated with the high-risk classification. Crucially, these sandboxes 
would serve as a collaborative platform, bringing together technology developers, certified 
OSH professionals, worker representatives, social partners, and national competent 
authorities—including representatives from bodies like EU-OSHA. This collaborative 
environment would allow for the

ation. The companies of the electrical and digital industry are expected to generate two thirds of 
their turnover with digitally enhanced products and digital services by 2026. For them, the deeper integration of

However, companies across all industry sectors face significant challenges due to several regulatory issues, in 
particular: an increasing volume of regulatory measures, a lack of coherence between different legislations, 
overlapping or duplicate regulations and dysfunctionalities in European digital policy. These challenges not only 
lead to legal uncertainties and associated administrative costs but also to unnecessary competitive 
disadvantages compared to companies in non-EU countries. 

Therefore, ZVEI welcomes the announcement of a digital package, the so-called “Digital Omnibus” to simplify 
EU digital regulations. ZVEI suggest the following measures in the areas of AI, cybersecurity and data usage to 
stimulate economic growth:

No extensive postponement of the AI Act 

As regards the current discussions on the AI Act, the TÜV Association calls for a timely application. We 

reject an extensive postponement as called for by various stakeholders. 

A delayed application of the AI Act would not only leave risks posed by AI systems unaddressed, but also 

hamper the quality-assured market placement of innovative AI systems, especially in high-risk areas. The 

agreed AI Act deadlines, as published in the EU’s Official Journal, ensure that both companies and 

designated authorities have legal and planning security to build up the necessary financial and human 

resources to ensure timely compliance readiness and a functioning AI market surveillance. If deadlines 

become negotiable, legislation becomes unpredictable, competition distorted, and apparent risks 

unaddressed – thus creating a harmful precedent.

reason that would justify a postponement. The AI Act, just as other Union product legislation, allows

Moreover, such a divergence between EU type-approval regulations and those of the UNECE frame-

Proposed simplification: AI requirements should be progressively incorporated into sectoral frame-
work, rather than applying immediately and in parallel with sectoral legislation, provided that the sec-
toral rules do not go beyond the current requirements of the AI Act. For these reasons, Annex I should 
be streamlined by merging its two sections and extending the more flexible Section B approach to the 
entire annex. This would ensure that AI requirements can be progressively incorporated into sectoral 
frameworks in a more stable and controlled manner, rather than applying immediately and in parallel 

6 

Simplifying the EU’s Digital Rulebook 

with sectoral legislation. Crucially, this would allow harmonised standards for AI to be translated and 
embedded into sector-specific contexts without undermining existing conformity procedures.1 

Integration of AI requirements into sectoral frameworks should follow a sequenced process grounded 
in existing legislation. The goal is not to reopen well-functioning regulatory systems, but to align them 
with the AI Act in a way that respects their structure and avoids legal uncertainty.  For this approach to 
succeed, the AI Act simplification package must clarify the AI Act’s status as a maximum harmonisation 
instrument. Sector-specific measures through delegated acts, implementing acts or technical specifi-
cations must not impose requirements beyond the AI Act. This is essential to prevent inconsistent or 
excessive obligations2, and would strengthen the replicability of AI harmonised standards, maintaining 
a unified definition of ‘state of the art’ across sectors.

conformity with the essential requirements of the CRA. These standards are not merely implementation 
tools. Rather, they are an integral legal basis for demonstrating compliance, especially for products 
with digital elements classified as “important” under Annex III (Class I), where third-party conformity 
assessment would otherwise be mandatory. 

Therefore, it must be ensured that a minimum of 36 months elapses between the formal publication of 
the relevant harmonised standards in the Official Journal of the European Union and the end of the 
transitional implementation period of the CRA. Only this  timeframe provides manufacturers with the 
necessary legal certainty and operational feasibility to meaningfully integrate the CRA requirements 
into product development and production processes. 

Tight  implementation  timelines  risk  negatively  impacting  existing  supply  chains.  Most  products  with
